abstract: <jats:p>Additional test-time computation can improve large language model (LLM) reasoning, but applying expensive inference uniformly to all queries is often inefficient. This paper studies cost-aware adaptive routing for budgeted LLM reasoning, where the system decides whether to keep a cheaper reasoning route or escalate to a more expensive revise-based route. We formulate this as an inference-time decision problem under an average-compute budget and evaluate it with an artifact-grounded pipeline built from stored outputs, oracle routing summaries, matched policy comparisons, and budget-aware analyses. Experiments on four 100-query GSM8K- and MATH-based regimes show that the usefulness of adaptive routing is strongly regime-dependent. In an easy GSM8K regime, revise-helpful cases are rare and routing headroom is limited. In harder GSM8K regimes, adaptive routing becomes substantially more useful: the best practical policy improves over the cheap baseline while using much less average computation than always revising, and in the strongest hard regime it matches the accuracy of always revising at markedly lower cost. Budget-aware analyses further show that modest cost increases can yield large gains when routing headroom is present, but that these gains quickly saturate. The results position adaptive routing as a practical inference-control mechanism for intelligent reasoning systems and highlight that effective test-time compute allocation depends on workload difficulty rather than uniform escalation.</jats:p>
title: When to Revise: Cost-Aware Adaptive Routing for LLM Reasoning

publisher: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)
title: PHI-GUARD: Compliance-Aware LLM Routing for Healthcare with Distribution-Free Safety Guarantees

abstract: <jats:p>The increasing use of large language models (LLMs) in enterprises creates a need for the effective selection between lower-cost models and more advanced ones. The aim of the article is to propose a multicriteria decision-making framework for prompt routing to LLMs in an enterprise environment, taking into account organizational preferences regarding cost, response quality, business risk, response time, standardization, and creativity. The study adopts a design-and-evaluation approach. In the design phase, a mechanism was developed in which prompts are assessed according to managerial routing criteria, weighted using the AHP method, and then directed to either a lower-cost or a more powerful model using the SAW method. In the evaluation phase, the solution was tested on a dataset of 100 business prompts and compared with two benchmark strategies: always cheap and always strong. The article’s contribution includes framing LLM routing as a managerial decision-support problem, operationalizing managerial routing criteria, and proposing evaluation metrics such as sufficiency rate, average cost per prompt, cost per sufficient response, and incremental cost of sufficiency gain. The results indicate that the proposed solution improves the cost–quality trade-off, while maintaining an acceptable level of response sufficiency and limiting the cost of query handling.</jats:p>
title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Framework for Enterprise LLM Routing

abstract: <jats:p>Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently been explored in evolutionary computation as generators of candidate solutions and even algorithm designers; however, their use as surrogate models for expensive black-box optimization remains underdeveloped, especially for lightweight open-source models that must balance accuracy and cost. This paper proposes an RL-enhanced framework that adapts a 7B instruction-tuned LLM into an effective surrogate for surrogate-assisted evolutionary algorithms (SAEAs). We construct an evolutionary-trajectory dataset from multiple benchmark suites and cast surrogate-assisted selection into two prompt-based tasks: fitness regression and promising-solution classification. The LLM is then fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using simple rule-based rewards that enforce structured outputs and improve prediction accuracy. The trained surrogates are integrated into an LLM-assisted EA that leverages in-context historical data and avoids repeated per-generation surrogate retraining. Extensive studies on SOP benchmarks and a dedicated offline testbed compare multiple mainstream LLMs and analyze the effects of dimensionality, context size, and training-data distributions. Results show that RL-trained lightweight LLM surrogates substantially improve selection accuracy and optimization performance, with classification-based surrogates consistently outperforming regression-based ones and achieving competitiveness with strong general-purpose LLMs at much lower cost.</jats:p>
title: Reinforcement Learning-Enabled LLM Surrogates in Evolutionary Algorithms
Adaptive Token Routing for Heterogeneous LLM Inference in Edge-Cloud Continuum
